metadata.title: Claude on the Clock.
metadata.description_blurb: Anthropic's Economic Index just got a heartbeat. 9,700 real users, linked to real usage logs — hourly cadences, artifact taxonomy, wage-token scatter, job-anxiety by perspective, and a survey on what people actually hope AI becomes. The most grounded picture of AI-at-work the field has produced. Liam, in for Bear.
metadata.source_chapter: Anthropic Economic Index: Cadences (June 2026) — Massenkoff, Lyubich, Sacher, Hitzig, Zhang, Heller, McCrory
metadata.source: Anthropic, 'Anthropic Economic Index report: Cadences', June 2026. Massenkoff, Lyubich, Sacher, Hitzig, Zhang, Heller, McCrory.
metadata.reformat: center-cut, focus-aware; pantry/<bid>-916.* is the human replacement slot; -916 overrides honored

beats.narration_text: What time is it in your city? Claude's logs already know. News at 7 a.m. Recipes at 6 p.m. Sleep advice at 5 a.m. Anthropic started sampling Claude usage hourly — and the economy's daily rhythm is etched right into it. I'm Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: What time is it in your city? Claude's logs already know. Anthropic just published the economy's daily heartbeat — and asked 9,700 people how AI actually feels at work.
output: 9,700 survey respondents · hourly telemetry · artifact taxonomy

beats.narration_text: One more data point. On April 14 — the day before the US tax deadline — tax-related conversations ran at eight times the average May day. April 15, just as high. April 16, sharp drop. The rest of the world's line stays flat underneath. Claude is a seasonal worker in the American tax system.

props.sparkLine: Eight times the average. One day.

beats.narration_text: 93 percent of Claude conversations produce a concrete output. Anthropic now names them: explanations, documents, guidance, apps, queries. Thirteen different output types. Personal use dominates creative writing, guidance, recipes. Work use dominates database queries, blogs, marketing. Plans and translation split almost evenly. The reclassification matters because the economics don't hide in whether you use Claude — they hide in what it makes.

Whose job changes? Everyone expects responsibility shifts — forty-six percent for themselves, fifty-five percent for juniors. Job loss is a different story. Only ten percent rate their own job loss as likely — slightly below the annualized US separation rate from all causes. But over a third put a junior colleague's odds above sixty percent. The oldest bias in forecasting: it'll happen to someone else. Specifically, someone junior.

One more split. Women are twelve percent of the linked sample. They delegate seven-point-three percentage points less, use Claude Code six-point-three points less. The one positive bar: active minutes — women log more. A signature of more iterative, collaborative use. Same tool, different mode.

props.sparkLine: More time. More iterative. More collaborative.

beats.narration_text: Last question in the survey: what do you hope an economy shaped by AI looks like in ten years? Over half: collaborate with AI on meaningful work. Just over fifty percent: AI takes the drudgery, more free time. A third: gains widely shared. Not replacement, not rescue. Collaboration, relief, a fair split. That is what the users are asking for.

Let's recap with Claude. Anthropic's Economic Index just got a heartbeat and a taxonomy. Hourly logs, named artifacts, and a survey of real users linked to real usage — this is the most grounded picture of how AI actually lands at work that the field has produced. The cadences are real, the economics track value, and the people who delegate the most are not the people who are afraid.

artifactLines: Usage mirrors the workweek: weekday backend work, weekend quant trading and agent design.
93% of conversations produce a named artifact; compute spend tracks occupational wage.
The product sets the leash length — Claude Code runs autonomously across 26 of 31 output types.
Expected progress is a rising tide — the same increment regardless of current exposure.
Those who delegate the most are the most optimistic on all six dimensions of job quality.

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude: 'What share of my work tasks could you do entirely on your own today — and which ones should I never hand over? Interview me, then give me your honest split.' Take this prompt, run it in your own session. What Claude says about your specific work tells you more than any survey. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: What share of my work tasks could you do entirely on your own today — and which ones should I never hand over? Interview me, then give me your honest split.

beats.narration_text: That's the short version. The full video also covers Your workweek and Claude's workweek…, Here is the economic signal.… and Same task, different leash. A… — watch Claude on the Clock. for the whole story. The link is right below.
